explicit_text: My father, Preston Lafayette Wright, was probably named after the general Lafayette. He served in the Confederate States Army and was a merchant and farmer who established a small bank in Johnston, South Carolina. His life and work were deeply rooted in the South's history and economy during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

explicit_text: I was a law student at the University of South Carolina around 1917 or 1918 and was involved in a student revolt to remove Dr. William S. Currell as president. The university was in decline after the dynamic leadership of Dr. S.C. Mitchell, and we wanted to restore its prestige. This experience was a defining moment in my early activism.

The university was in decline after the dynamic leadership of his predecessor, and we wanted to restore its prestige. This experience was a defining moment in my early activism.

explicit_text: Dr. William S. Currell was the president of the University of South Carolina during my student years, succeeding Dr. S.C. Mitchell. Many students, including myself, felt he lacked the magnetism and leadership to maintain the university's prestige, leading to a petition for his removal. Although he gave a strong speech and stayed on for a while, our opposition marked a significant episode in university history.

explicit_text: Dr. S.C. Mitchell was the dynamic president of the University of South Carolina before Dr. Currell, and under his leadership, the university reached its greatest prestige. explicit_text: In my youth, I worked as a clerk in a store in Trenton, South Carolina, owned by Walter Wise. There I witnessed a disturbing incident where a well-dressed black man was chased out of the store for using a washbasin, highlighting the racial hostility of the time. This event deeply affected my views on race relations.

Aiken represented a point of contact between northern and southern social dynamics.
